attributes.stem:
A 37-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of joint pain. She reports bilateral swelling and stiffness of her knuckles, particularly worse in the mornings lasting approximately two hours before improving. She has not noticed any rashes or eye pain. Her medical history includes being a 15 pack-year smoker and carpal tunnel release surgery on her left hand one year ago. She does not take any regular medications and does not drink alcohol.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands with sparing of the distal interphalangeal joints. Recent blood tests show a raised anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody, negative double-stranded DNA antibody, and liver function tests within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate disease-modifying agent to initiate alongside short-term glucocorticoids?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features highly suggestive of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), including symmetrical small joint polyarthritis, morning stiffness, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. The most appropriate first-line disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drug (DMARD) for RA is methotrexate, which should be initiated early in the disease course to prevent joint damage and disability.

Methotrexate is the anchor drug in RA treatment due to its established efficacy, acceptable safety profile, and low cost. Approximately 25-40% of patients improve with methotrexate monotherapy, with response rates increasing to around 50% when combined with glucocorticoids. While methotrexate is contraindicated during pregnancy and requires effective contraception, this does not preclude its use in women of childbearing age. Rather, it necessitates appropriate counseling about pregnancy prevention during treatment and for at least 6 months after discontinuation. The decision to use methotrexate should be individualized, weighing benefits against risks after thorough discussion with the patient about her reproductive plans.

Ibuprofen is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug that may provide symptomatic relief but does not modify the disease course or prevent joint damage in RA. It is not classified as a DMARD and would be inadequate as monotherapy for newly diagnosed RA.

Leflunomide is an alternative DMARD that may be considered when methotrexate is contraindicated or not tolerated. However, it is not typically used as first-line therapy and also carries pregnancy risks with an even longer washout period than methotrexate.

Sulfasalazine is another DMARD option for RA but is generally less effective than methotrexate as monotherapy. It may be used in patients with mild disease or as part of combination therapy, but would not be the first choice for this patient with features suggesting potentially aggressive disease.

Hydroxychloroquine is a DMARD with a more favorable safety profile but modest efficacy in RA when used alone. It is often used as part of combination therapy or in very mild disease. It has a significant role in systemic lupus erythematosus, which is less likely in this patient given the negative double-stranded DNA antibody.

Current guidelines emphasize early aggressive treatment with DMARDs to achieve remission or low disease activity. Methotrexate combined with short-term glucocorticoids provides the best chance of controlling disease activity quickly while minimizing long-term joint damage.
learningPoints: Methotrexate combined with short-term glucocorticoids is the first-line management for rheumatoid arthritis.
Methotrexate is contraindicated during pregnancy and requires effective contraception, but this does not preclude its use in women of childbearing age after appropriate counseling.
Early aggressive treatment with disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs improves long-term outcomes in rheumatoid arthritis.
references: Mian A, Ibrahim F, Scott DL. A systematic review of guidelines for managing rheumatoid arthritis. BMC Rheumatology 2019; 3:42-55.
